onet2r 0.2.0

Getting Started with onet2r

Source: vignettes/getting-started.Rmd
getting-started.Rmd
library(onet2r)
library(dplyr)
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'dplyr'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
#> 
#>     filter, lag
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(tibble)

has_onet_key <- nzchar(Sys.getenv("ONET_API_KEY"))
run_live <- has_onet_key && identical(Sys.getenv("ONET2R_RUN_LIVE_VIGNETTES"), "true")

onet2r helps you move from occupation search to analysis-ready O*NET tables. The Web Services API requires a free API key from the O*NET developer portal. Store it in .Renviron as ONET_API_KEY=your-api-key-here so you do not put secrets in scripts.

This article uses small representative tibbles so the pkgdown site always shows output. To run the same chunks against the live API while building locally, set both ONET_API_KEY and ONET2R_RUN_LIVE_VIGNETTES=true.

tibble(
  setting = c("ONET_API_KEY configured", "live vignette calls enabled"),
  value = c(has_onet_key, run_live)
)
#> # A tibble: 2 × 2
#>   setting                     value
#>   <chr>                       <lgl>
#> 1 ONET_API_KEY configured     TRUE 
#> 2 live vignette calls enabled FALSE

Search for Occupations

Search is usually the first step. Results include an O*NET-SOC code and an occupation title.

software_jobs <- if (run_live) {
  onet_search("software developer", start = 1, end = 5)
} else {
  tibble(
    code = c("15-1252.00", "15-1251.00", "15-2051.00"),
    title = c(
      "Software Developers",
      "Computer Programmers",
      "Data Scientists"
    )
  )
}

head(software_jobs, 5)
#> # A tibble: 3 × 2
#>   code       title               
#>   <chr>      <chr>               
#> 1 15-1252.00 Software Developers 
#> 2 15-1251.00 Computer Programmers
#> 3 15-2051.00 Data Scientists

Retrieve Occupation Details

Most detail endpoints return tibbles. That makes them easy to inspect, join, and summarize.

skills <- if (run_live) {
  onet_skills("15-1252.00", start = 1, end = 5)
} else {
  tibble(
    id = c("2.A.1.a", "2.A.1.b", "2.B.3.m"),
    name = c(
      "Reading Comprehension",
      "Active Listening",
      "Programming"
    ),
    description = c(
      "Understanding written sentences and paragraphs in work-related documents.",
      "Giving full attention to what other people are saying.",
      "Writing computer programs for various purposes."
    ),
    value = c(4.12, 4.00, 4.38),
    scale = c("Importance", "Importance", "Importance")
  )
}

head(skills, 5)
#> # A tibble: 3 × 5
#>   id      name                  description                          value scale
#>   <chr>   <chr>                 <chr>                                <dbl> <chr>
#> 1 2.A.1.a Reading Comprehension Understanding written sentences and…  4.12 Impo…
#> 2 2.A.1.b Active Listening      Giving full attention to what other…  4    Impo…
#> 3 2.B.3.m Programming           Writing computer programs for vario…  4.38 Impo…

The same pattern works for knowledge, abilities, tasks, work activities, work styles, work context, interests, technology, education, and job zones.

Work with O*NET Tables

The O*NET database tables expose lower-level source data. Use onet_tables() to find available tables and onet_table_info() to inspect columns before downloading a full table.

tables <- if (run_live) {
  onet_tables()
} else {
  tibble(
    id = c("Occupation Data", "Abilities", "Skills", "Task Ratings"),
    title = c("Occupation Data", "Abilities", "Skills", "Task Ratings")
  )
}

head(tables, 6)
#> # A tibble: 4 × 2
#>   id              title          
#>   <chr>           <chr>          
#> 1 Occupation Data Occupation Data
#> 2 Abilities       Abilities      
#> 3 Skills          Skills         
#> 4 Task Ratings    Task Ratings
table_info <- if (run_live) {
  onet_table_info("Abilities")
} else {
  tibble(
    name = c("onetsoc_code", "element_id", "scale_id", "data_value"),
    title = c("O*NET-SOC Code", "Element ID", "Scale ID", "Data Value"),
    type = c("character", "character", "character", "decimal"),
    format = c(NA, NA, NA, "n.n")
  )
}

head(table_info, 8)
#> # A tibble: 4 × 4
#>   name         title          type      format
#>   <chr>        <chr>          <chr>     <chr> 
#> 1 onetsoc_code O*NET-SOC Code character NA    
#> 2 element_id   Element ID     character NA    
#> 3 scale_id     Scale ID       character NA    
#> 4 data_value   Data Value     decimal   n.n

Join Labor-Market Context

O*NET describes what occupations do. BLS OEWS adds employment and wage scale. This example uses the package’s bundled OEWS sample so it always runs.

sample_oews <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "oews-national-sample.csv",
  package = "onet2r"
)

oews <- onet_oews_national(2024, path = sample_oews)

occupations <- tibble(
  code = c("15-1252.00", "29-1141.00", "11-1011.00"),
  title = c("Software Developers", "Registered Nurses", "Chief Executives")
)

occupation_wages <- onet_join_oews(occupations, oews = oews)
head(occupation_wages, 5)
#> # A tibble: 3 × 9
#>   code       title  soc_code  year oews_type occ_title tot_emp a_median h_median
#>   <chr>      <chr>  <chr>    <int> <chr>     <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 15-1252.00 Softw… 15-1252   2024 national  Software… 1847900   133080     64.0
#> 2 29-1141.00 Regis… 29-1141   2024 national  Register… 3175400    93070     44.8
#> 3 11-1011.00 Chief… 11-1011   2024 national  Chief Ex…  211230   206680     99.4

Summarize Weighted O*NET Records

Employment-weighted summaries are useful when the same descriptor appears across occupations with very different labor-market sizes.

skill_rows <- tibble(
  code = c("15-1252.00", "15-1252.00", "29-1141.00", "29-1141.00"),
  element_id = c("2.A.1.a", "2.A.1.b", "2.A.1.a", "2.A.1.b"),
  element_name = c(
    "Reading Comprehension",
    "Active Listening",
    "Reading Comprehension",
    "Active Listening"
  ),
  data_value = c(4.12, 4.00, 3.88, 4.25)
)

weighted_skills <- onet_weighted_summary(
  skill_rows,
  group = c("element_id", "element_name"),
  value = "data_value",
  oews = oews
)

head(weighted_skills, 5)
#> # A tibble: 2 × 7
#>   element_id element_name     n_records n_occupations total_weight weighted_mean
#>   <chr>      <chr>                <int>         <int>        <dbl>         <dbl>
#> 1 2.A.1.a    Reading Compreh…         2             2      5023300          3.97
#> 2 2.A.1.b    Active Listening         2             2      5023300          4.16
#> # ℹ 1 more variable: wage_weighted_mean <dbl>

Build Longitudinal Archive Comparisons

The API serves the current release. To compare releases over time, use the archive helpers. See the longitudinal articles for the methodological cautions.

panel <- tibble(
  release_version = rep(c("1.0", "2.0"), each = 2),
  release_date = rep(as.Date(c("2020-01-01", "2021-01-01")), each = 2),
  soc_vintage = "2019",
  domain = "Abilities",
  onet_soc_code = rep(c("15-1252.00", "29-1141.00"), 2),
  soc_code = rep(c("15-1252", "29-1141"), 2),
  title = rep(c("Software Developers", "Registered Nurses"), 2),
  element_id = "1.A.1.a.1",
  element_name = "Oral Comprehension",
  scale_id = "IM",
  data_value = c(4.0, 4.2, 4.4, 4.2),
  source_date = as.Date(c("2020-01-01", "2020-01-01", "2021-01-01", "2020-01-01")),
  domain_source = "Analyst"
)

bridge <- tibble(
  from_vintage = "2019",
  to_vintage = "2019",
  from_soc_code = c("15-1252", "29-1141"),
  to_soc_code = c("15-1252", "29-1141"),
  from_title = c("Software Developers", "Registered Nurses"),
  to_title = c("Software Developers", "Registered Nurses"),
  step_count = 0L,
  map_type = "one_to_one",
  crosswalk_weight = 1
)

changes <- onet_panel_reconcile(panel, bridge)
head(changes, 5)
#> # A tibble: 2 × 26
#>   from_release to_release from_release_date to_release_date from_soc_code
#>   <chr>        <chr>      <date>            <date>          <chr>        
#> 1 1.0          2.0        2020-01-01        2021-01-01      15-1252      
#> 2 1.0          2.0        2020-01-01        2021-01-01      29-1141      
#> # ℹ 21 more variables: to_soc_code <chr>, soc_vintage_from <chr>,
#> #   soc_vintage_to <chr>, domain <chr>, element_id <chr>, element_name <chr>,
#> #   scale_id <chr>, from_value <dbl>, to_value <dbl>, value_change <dbl>,
#> #   value_percent_change <dbl>, from_source_date <date>, to_source_date <date>,
#> #   date_changed <lgl>, value_changed <lgl>, change_type <fct>,
#> #   method_break <lgl>, crosswalk_uncertain <lgl>, safely_comparable <lgl>,
#> #   map_type <chr>, crosswalk_weight <dbl>

Next Steps

  • Read vignette("longitudinal-onet-background", package = "onet2r") before interpreting cross-release changes.
  • Read vignette("longitudinal-archives", package = "onet2r") to assemble and reconcile archive panels.
  • Read vignette("oews-wage-context", package = "onet2r") to add BLS wage and employment estimates.

On this page
